segment-text: UNCLE BUCK
EXT. CHICAGO SUBURBS. HOUSE. AFTERNOON

A 40's Georgian colonial in an established North Shore suburb. It's late autumn. WE HEAR A SINGLE HEARTBEAT. A TEENAGE girl crosses the lawn on the bias, dragging her feet, head hung enveloped in thought. To the front door. Her name is TIA RUSSELL. CU. PURSE Tia digs through her purse, coming up with a key ring.
INT. FOYER

Tia lets herself in and takes off her coat. She's not a happy girl. Very pretty, very mature but stricken with a look of tragic preoccupation. Her clothes are dark and somber. Attractive and stylish in their own way but exclusionary. WE HEAR ANOTHER HEARTBEAT.
EXT. HOUSE. STREET

A yellow school van pulls up in front of the house and a six year old girl gets out. Her name is MAIZY RUSSELL. She's dainty and proper. A girl like they don't make anymore. She's wearing a raincoat and huge backpack. She marches up to the house, careful not to step on any cracks. WE HEAR ANOTHER HEARTBEAT.
EXT. BACKYARD

An eight year old boy climbs over a back fence and scopes out the yard. Considering it safe, he makes a dash for the house. His name is MILES RUSSELL. He's the sort of chipped-tooth scraped-knee boy that grows up between two sisters. He's filthy and torn from a perilous journey home. His Air Jordans are scuffed and trailing splayed laces, his jeans are hanging low in the back. His backpack's split and threatening to spill the books and crumpled papers it contains. ANOTHER HEARTBEAT.
INT. KITCHEN
Tia's drinking a small bottle of Evian. Maizy drops her backpack on the table and takes off her coat.
Get your backpack off the
table. People eat there.
They eat on plates and the
plates are on the table.
Don't give me any crap, Maizy.
I'm telling you said crap.
There's nothing wrong with crap.
(surprised) Really? I thought that was a swear.
You're thinking of "shit".
Oh, right.
The back door flies open and Miles bursts in.
Do you mind?
A sixth grader chased me on his bike and I was running and when I got exhausted and fell down, he wailed on me
with his shoe.
You can thank your parents for that.
Howcome?
It was their brilliant idea to move here. They weren't making enough cash in Indianapolis, forget that we were perfectly happy. So, thank them for getting treated like shit every day.
I'm telling on that one.
Shut your face.
He peels off his backpack and drops it by the door.
That doesn't go there.
He looks down at the backpack. The floor by the door seems as good a place as any.
Oh, let's have a cow.
He starts to exit the room. Tia grabs him.
Your nails are digging into my arm, goddamn it!
Pick it up!
She releases him and he picks up the backpack.
(indignant) You're just supposed to open the door for us. You're not supposed to kick us around.
Maizy? Did I kick you around?
No, but you said "shit'' twice.
Tia gives Maizy an impatient look. Maizy qualifies her remark.
But only once for real.
(to Miles) I got better things to do than babysit you, you little stain.
Oh, like what? Hang out with the friends you don't have?
Tia glares at Miles. He's hit a sore point with her.
Shut-up.
You want to make me?
He crosses the kitchen on his way out.
When our mother-figure isn't here, I'm in charge.
Miles stops and turns to Tia.
I'm sick of you calling her that!
He exits angrily. ANOTHER HEARTBEAT. AND ANOTHER. Maizy looks at Tia.
I don't know why we need boys at all. They're so loud.
We need boys so they can get married and turn into
shadows.
Let the dog out. She exits leaving a severely confused Maizy. A PAIR OF HEARTBEATS.
INT. KITCHEN. CU. CHINESE FOOD CONTAINERS. NIGHT
Opened carry-out Chinese boxes. A COUPLE MORE HEARTBEATS.
INT. DINING ROOM. LATER

BOB and CINDY RUSSELL are home from work. The family's eating dinner. Bob's in his early forties, handsome, trim and healthy. A classic Reagan Era father. Cindy is in her late thirties. She's attractive and youthful, strong and successful. The Russells are the modern two income family. Bob's at the head of the table. Cindy's opposite him. Maizy and Miles are on one side of the table. Tia's alone on the other. It's not the warmest domestic scene in town. There's a peculiar stiffness to their interaction. A PAIR OF HEARTBEATS.
Miles?
He looks from his plate.
Huh?
Did you win at basketball today?
It's tomorrow.
She looks at Cindy with a smug smile. ANOTHER PAIR OF HEARTBEATS She knows that Tia's mocking her but lets it pass. ANOTHER PAIR OF HEARTBEATS.
INT. DINING ROOM
Bob senses impending difficulties. He picks up the conversation.
You'll win. And next week you have a birthday.
Miles smiles.
What about me?
Your birthday's in June.
When Dad goes to New York, I'm taking the week off work.
INT, DINING ROOM. TIA'S LAP. CU. FINGER
She makes a circle in the air with her index finger. A secret insult to Cindy. ANOTHER PAIR OF HEARTBEATS.
EXT. HOUSE. NIGHT
The living room lights go off. The HEARTBEATS BECOME REGULAR AND RHYTHMIC AND CONTINUE. The porch lights go out.
INT. KITCHEN. DOGGIE DOOR

The family's miniature poodle scoots in the doggie door. CU. ALARM CLOCK Cindy sets it. CU. MAIZY She's as~eep in bed. The HEART SKIPS A BEAT AND CONTINUES AT A FASTER RATE. CU. FRONT DOOR. LOCK Bob secures it for the night. He's sprawled across his bed, sleeping. The HEART SKIPS
INT, MASTER BEDROOM
Tastefully decorated. Bob and Cindy are in their iron bed reading business related material. They're wearing halfglasses. The HEART'S TRYING TO GET BACK IN RHYTHM. IT'S BEATING FASTER.
EXT. HOUSE. LATER
The lights are out. The house is asleep. The HEAR.T'S POUNDING, WILDLY ARHYTHMIC. And ... IT STOPS. In bed, sleeping. Her eyes open -- confused and frightened.
INT. MASTER BEDROOM
It's dark. The phone's ringing. Bob and Cindy stir. Bob reaches out and picks up the phone.
(groggy) Hello?
He comes quickly to his senses and sits up. Cindy rises up on her elbows.
Oh, God ...
(frightened) What?
Just a second. (covers the phone, to Cindy) It's your aunt.
What happened?
Your Dad had a heart attack. CU. CINDY
She's breathless with shock.
INT. KITCHEN. LATER
Bob's leaning against the counter. Cindy's slumped in a kitchen chair, devasted. She's staring at the floor.
Your Dad's a strong, healthy guy. He's gonna be alright.
Cindy looks up at him. to be cheered up. A tinge of anger. She doesn't want
I believe it.
Who's going to watch the kids?
Marcie? She's right next door ...
She's the last person I'd ask for a favor.
What about the Neville's?
Cindy thinks for a moment. She nods. A good thought.
Would you call? I want to leave as soon as we can. (pause) If we were home, I'd be there. I'm so goddamn
helpless here.
Don't start getting down on why we moved. It has nothing to do with what happened.
Cindy knows he's right. She recants.
What about my brother? I don't know the Neville's from Adam. I'm sure he'd be glad to help out.
He doesn't have kids, he isn't even married.
He's a little out there but he's responsible and he's family.
Call the Neville's.
Cindy exits. Bob crosses to the phone.
INT. HALLWAY
Cindy comes up the stairs. She stops as she looks up. HER POV Tia's standing at the top of the stairs. and panties, hair pinned up.
What happened to Grandpa?
She's in at-shirt
She's surprised that Tia knows about it. Stern, serious and tough as a slab of pig iron.
And don't lie to me.
There's obviously a fissure in their relationship. hurt by the remark.
Still hard and cold.
I heard you talking.
INT. HALLWAY
Cindy continues up the stairs to Tia.
He had a heart attack. Tia's jaw tightens.
But he's okay.
You can't have a heart attack and be okay.
Honey, I don't know.
Are we going to Indianapolis?
Daddy and I are.
And we're not?
No.
Cindy's
She turns around and heads back to her room.
I love my father very much.
Tia turns.
So, why did you move away from him? If my whole family moved away on me, I'd have a heart attack, too.
She goes into her bedroom and slams the door.
INT. BEDROOM
Cindy is loading clothes into an open suitcase on the bed.
INT. ROOM. DOORWAY
Miles is standing in the doorway. his sleepy ass. Hair tossled, scratching
What're you doing? Who slammed the door? Are you going somewhere? What time is it?
INT. CINDY
She turns from the bed to Miles.
Come here.
Miles walks over to her. She sits down and puts Miles on her lap.
Grandpa's not feeling real good, so Dad and I are going down to see him.
Is he sick?
Sort of.
With what?
He's just a little sick, honey, and he wants to see me and Dad.
Who's going to take care of us?
Mr. and Mrs. Neville.
Is that a joke?
No.
You don't like the Neville's?
Their dog's a ball sniffer.
Don't talk like that.
Mr. Neville yelled at Michael Larson because their dog was sniffing Michael's balls.
Don't use that word.
I don't know another word.
I'm sure we can talk to Mr. Neville about the dog ...
INT. ROOM. DOOR
Bob's in the doorway.
Don't bother.
She looks past Miles.
He shakes his head.
They're in Florida.
She sighs in defeat.
(to Miles) You get in bed. You have to get up for school.
INT. BEDROOM
Bob steps in. Miles turns to him.
Who's in Florida?
Just get back in bed.
What's the other word for balls?
Get in bed.
I think Tia slammed her door
Now!
Miles hurries out of the room. Cindy looks up at Bob.
What do we do?
I don't think we have much choice.
(cautiously) Can we trust him?
Bob nods with great certainty.
Oh, yeah. Sure. He's my brother, for God's sake. And believe me, to help us out, he'll drop everything.
CU. FLOOR
In a darkened room. A booze bottle, a filled ashtray, a dinner plate, plastic cups, an alarm clock, the base portion of a telephone and finally a WOMAN crashes to the floor.
INT. BEDROOM
Lit by a beer sign on the wall. BUCK RUSSELL sits up in bed. He's wearing at-shirt. His thinning hair's standing on end. He's holding the phone wrong way up.
Yo!

The woman, CHANICE BLACKWELL, picks herself up off the floor. She's still half-asleep. She's in her late thirties, an attractive and independent woman. She's tough and a little hard in manner and look. Not so much cheap or easy as strong and pragmatic.
What's going on?
Hello?
INT. BEDROOM. BOB
He's sitting on the edge of the bed. In the background, Cindy continues packing.
Buck? This is Bob. CU. BUCK
We see Buck in glorious detail. Bloodshot eyes, a couple days growth of beard.
(thinks) Bob who?
CU. BOB He's embarrassed that Buck doesn't recognize him immediately. He flashes a look to Cindy before speaking.
(softly) Your brother.
INT. BUCK'S BEDROOM
Chanice slips back into bed. Buck brightens as he realizes it's his older brother. He clears his throat, setting off the neighborhood dogs in a barking frenzy.
Bobby! What's going on? What time is it? CU. BOB
Bob gets to the heart of the matter.
( serious) I need a favor.
CU. BUCK He's puzzled.
I haven't talked to you in a long time. We have to get together. You're living here now, I'm living here. CU. BOB
He cuts Buck off.
Cindy's father had a heart attack tonight. CU. BUCK
The goofy grin drops. He feels foolish for being so exuberant when there's such a grave problem.
She's packing the suitcases. She looks up at Bob, hoping they're doing the right thing. CU. BOB He explains the favor he needs.
We want to get down to Indianapolis as soon as possible. But we're stuck for somebody to watch the kids.
INT . BED!;HJOM
Chanice is curious about the change in Buck's attitude.
Who is it?
Buck waves her off. He's focused on the conversation and is overly sharp with her.
Oh, sure. Do we want to do this in the morning or what?
(interrupts) You start work in the morning.
Buck waves her off again. She's angry. She doesn't realize the seriousness of the matter.
Tonight? (pauses, thinks) Sure.
Chanice glares at him. unorthodox behavior.
I'll grab a clean pair of scivies and be on my way. (pause) Tell Cindy I'm sorry.
Buck swings his legs over the side of the bed and hangs up the phone. Chanice is still staring at him. He sits for a moment, letting it all sink in.
(bitterly) So much for your promises.
(annoyed) My sister-in-law's father had a heart attack. Chanice doesn't respond. It's something she can't complain about.
She has to separate the event from the larger issues.
I'm sorry to hear that.
Buck gets out of bed and crosses to his closet.
They need me to watch their kids .
So, you're not starting work tomorrow?
I don't see how I can.
(facetiously) And you seem real disappointed. You were probably the first call because he knows you don't work.
Buck comes out of the closet with an armful of shirts and pants.
I work, okay? I just don't work like you work.
Gambling is not work.
Gambling is a hobby.
Oh, sorry. What's your work then?
Pardon me. I don't sell goddamn tires. Which is, in your book, the most noble thing in the world. And anybody who doesn't do it is a bum.
Buck pulls a cotton laundry bag out from under his bed and begins filling it with clothing.
Buck, I wouldn't care if I knew you were sincere about me and about helping make some kind of future for us.
Buck crosses to the bed and grabs his pillow. He pulls the pillow out of the case and fills the case with socks and shorts.
Working at your place is a future, huh? My wife, my boss.
My husband, my partner.
(pause) Yeah, right. Maybe this is for the best. Maybe we need to cool things off a little bit.
He crosses back to the laundry bag and takes out a pair of sweat pants. He slips them on.

Or, after five years, maybe we need to quit kidding ourselves. I don't have the spare years laying around anymore that I can spend on somebody who won't and maybe can't get serious about himself or anyone else.
It's your call.
It's that easy for you to say?
He reaches under the chair for a shirt. He quickly checks it for excessive dirt. It's alright. He slips it on.
If you're not staying, lock up. I gotta go. There's two legitimately unhappy people waiting for me.
(softer) Call me if you change your mind about the job or anything else.
You do the same. The number's in my book.
He starts to exit.
Buck? If you need help ...
If I can't handle a couple of kids for a few days, you're right about me. And he's gone.
She sighs in defeat. As much as she loves him, she knows he
EXT. STREET
A rusting 1969 Buick Riviera blasts through a suburban
intersection. Music's blasting. INT. HOUSE. LIVING ROOM
Cindy's dressed and standing at the front window. Bob comes down the stairs.
She won't open the door.
I don't know what her problem is.
Miles is fine. He didn't remember Buck but it's okay.
He's just glad the Nevilles
oldest boy terrorizes him.
That's not what he told me.
Are you sure Buck knows his
EXT. HOUSE
Buck's car's in the driveway. He's standing at the doorway with his pillow and a grocery bag. He rings the bell for the umpteenth time.
ANYBODY HOME?
A porch light goes on.
EXT. HOUSE. FRONT DOOR
The door opens, revealing a sleepy, puzzled ELDERLY WOMAN. CU. BUCK He's startled. He leans back and looks at the house address. He looks back at the woman.
One of us is at the wrong house.
EXT. BOB'S HOUSE
Bob's standing in front of the house, looking down the street. We HEAR A CAR ENGINE. CU. BOB He's looking down the street.
EXT. HOUSE. STREET
The Buick roars through the FRAME. OC TIRES SQUEAL. The Buick backs into FRAME and stops. Buck gets out and looks at Bob's house.
Bob?
INT. KITCHEN
Buck's in the kitchen with Cindy. She's running down the basics of the house and schedule.
The kids are going to tell you ninety percent of what you need to know anyway. They can take care of themselves for the most part.
Right.
You have to drive Tia to school in the morning. And Miles and Maizy.
Maizy's the dog?
She's the six year old.
Right.
If things aren't going smoothly, take them to the mall and let them buy something. I know it sounds horrible but it works.
Cindy's remark strikes him as odd. This is contrary to what little he knows about raising kids. He nods in agreement only to cover his discomfort. Bob comes in from the dining room.
We better hit the road.
Okay. Buck, thanks a million.
My best to your family.
Cindy exits. Bob hangs back for a moment
You're alright with this?
I'm fine. Get outta here.
Bob exits. Buck watches for a moment before closing the door.
INT. HOUSE, HALLWAY. LATER
Tia slips out of her room and sneaks down the hall.
INT. MASTER BATHROOM

Buck is in his shorts, brushing his teeth in the high-style bathroom. Every possible gadget and accessory. Granite counters, towel warmers, bidet, two-person whirlpool tub, steam shower. Buck flips on a 3 inch screen TV mounted on a bracket on the sink. He's not entirely sure why a TV would be in a bathroom. He crosses to the toilet and sits down. He leans forward and looks at the TV. He gets up and steps into the tub. He sits down and looks at the TV from there.
INT. MASTER BEDROOM
Tia peeks into the bedroom. She listens to the running water in the bathroom. She slips into the bedroom.
INT. SHOWER
Buck, with the toothbrush in his mouth, looks out from the shower to the TV.
INT. BATHROOM. DOOR
Tia's spying on Buck between the space between the hinges and the jamb.
INT. BATHROOM. BUCK
He exits the shower and crosses to a exercise treadmill. He puzzles over it for a moment. He steps up on it and studies the control panel. He takes the toothbrush out of his mouth.
CU. PANEL
A confusion of touch-pads and displays. Buck's finger presses a touchpad.
INT. BATHROOM
The treadmill suddenly comes ~o life. The walk surface moves out from under him, and in the blink of an eye, he's thrown to his knees and hurled off the machine. The toothbrush goes flying.
CU. TOILET BOWL
Buck's toothbrush hits the seat and drops in the bowl. Tom Jones' "DELILAH" COMES UP AND CONTINUES.
EXT. HOUSE. MORNING
A crisp late autumn morning. The MUSIC CONTINUES.
INT. HOUSE. STAIRS
Tia comes down the stairs dressed for school. Her outfit is gloomy and extreme. She slows as she hears an OFF-KEY SINGING VOICE FOLLOWING THE SONG.
INT. KITCHEN
Buck's in sweats and at-shirt. He's at the stove cooking, moving to the music, the source of which is a Proton radio on the counter.
INT. KITCHEN. DOOR

Tia stands in the doorway. She looks Buck up and down. HER POV. CU. BUCK'S FEET Grotesquely misshapen and worn, laceless gum sole, canvas top shoes and black socks. MOVING UP the sweat pants to the blown-out seat, to the t-shirt and ENDING on thinning hair standing on end. A spatula COMES INTO FRAME and with the handle end, Buck scratches the top of his head. Tia turns it off. He freezes in mid-note. Thinks a moment and turns. Heavy, inpenetrable, inscrutable, teenage "I hate you'' scowl. Through the scowl he sees the sweet face of his brother's first born, not the petulant, distressed young woman she's become.
Tia?
No reply. No response. She wants to let him know that she's a
INT. KITCHEN
Buck takes the frying pan he's been attending from the burner.
Do you remember me?
Tia ignores him. She opens a cupboard and removes a coffee mug.
Uncle Buck? :Irua. Uncle Buck?
Tia continues to ignore him as she pours herself a cup of Buck's freshly manufactured coffee matter. Buck begins to catch on to her gig.
Coffee drinker, huh?
Tia sits down at the table.
Hungry?
No.
She sips the coffee. She struggles to repress her urge to grimace. Either she doesn't normally drink coffee or his coffee's horrible.
He notes her_displeasure with the coffee, despite her efforts to conceal it.
Where're the other ones?
Looks up with loads of boredom and irritation.
Other ones what?
INT. KITCHEN
Buck pulls a carving knife from a holder beside the stove.
The other kids.
He takes a grapefruit from a fruit bowl on the counter.
They have names. Miles and Maizy.
Are Miles and Maizv awake yet?
I woke them, thank you.

Buck flips the grapefruit in the air and swings at it with the knife. Two perfect halves of the grapefruit fall on the counter. She's disgusted. He retrieves the halves, notes the clean, even cut and plants a half on a plate. He spoons a load of eggs onto the plate.
I'm new to this parenting game.
INT. KITCHEN
Buck drops a piece of toast on the plate.
(facetiously) Oh, really? I couldn't tell.
Buck ignores the remark and sets the plate before her.
Are you deaf? I said I wasn't hungry.
(making cheerful) This is one of my specialties.
Tia looks down at the plate. The grapefruit half, very dry scrambled eggs with bits of bologna, chunks of onions and green pepper.
INT. KITCHEN
Tia pushes the plate away.
I'd rather starve, thank you.
Your call. Does your Mom know you drink coffee?
I'm not doing it to impress you.
And I appreciate it. (pause) Any particular reason why you're giving me a hard time? Am
I giving you a hard time?
Miles walks in. He jumps back in shock at seeing Buck.
Who're you!?
I'm your Uncle Buck.
Miles looks at Tia.
Is this true?
Unfortunately.
You're taking care of us?
Lord and Master of this joint until the folks return. We're gonna get along great and we're gonna have a swell time. Where's ... ?
Her name is Maizy. For the second time.
Buck gives Tia a look. He doesn't care for her mouth. He serves Miles. Miles stares at the food.
What's this?
Army eggs.
Oh, my God! (to Tia) He put onions in the eggs.
So?
Tia gets up from the table.
Have some cereal. on Maizy.
She exits. Buck walks back to the stove.
Is your sister always this pleasant?
(thinks for a moment) No. She's usually in a bad mood in the morning.
INT. HOUSE. MAIZY'S ROOM
Tia and Maizy are sitting on the bed. Tia's combing Maizy's hair. Maizy is whining and squirming.
You want knots in your hair?
Ow!
Cut it out!
I want Mom to do it.
Mom's not here.
She is, too.
No, she's not. She and Dad went to Indianapolis.
They did not.
Tia stops combing.
Okay, they didn't.
Maizy turns to Tia with surprise.
They did?
Yes. And I'm taking care of you.
She looks at **Tia with alarm.
But you can't drive!
INT. KITCHEN
Buck's sitting at the table with Miles. They're picking the bologna, peppers and onions out of the eggs.
Where do you live?
In the city.
What do you do?
Lots of things.
Where's your office?
Buck hesistates, not having an office.
I don't have one.
Howcome?
I just don't.
Where's your wife?
(after a pause) I haven't found her yet.
Do you have kids?
Just myself.
Are you my Dad's brother?
What's your record for consecutives questions asked?
Huh?
Nothing. Yeah, I'm your Dad's brother.
Miles nods, satisfied with the answer.
You have much more hair in your nose than my Dad.
Why, thank you.
Tia walks into the kitchen. Maizy follows. cupboard and takes out a box of cereal. Tia goes to the
(to Maizy) Hi, there.
Maizy stares at him.
Say hello, Maizy.
Hello.
I'm your Uncle Buck.
Maizy, sit down.
Maizy climbs onto a chair. Tia serves her a bowl of cereal.
What time do we have to shove off for school?
Miles starts at 8:45. Maizy goes at noon.
And you?
I'm not going to school.
Pardon me? I'm watching Maizy.
That's not what your mother said.
That's tough.
It sure is because you're going to school.
Tia sits down at the table.
Is that a fact?
That is a fact, that is a reality and that is a foregone conclusion.
And how are you going to accomplish that?
Well, if I can't persuade you, I can sure as heck tie you up and throw your snotty little butt in the trunk of my car.
Tia leans back in surprise.
INT. CAR. LATER
Buck's driving, Tia next to him, Miles and Maizy are in the backseat. Tia's fiddling with the radio. The car's vibrating, rattling and howling with muffler leakage.
Who can guess who was president when this car was manufactured?
Abraham Lincoln,
Wrong. Miles? It smells like carbon monoxide in here.
Carbon monoxide is odorless, Miles. That's why it's so dangerous. What you're smelling is burning oil.
Is the radio busted?
Just give it a whack,
What?
Punch it. Right on the dash. Give it a whack.
Tia reluctantly smacks the dashboard.
Come on, you can do better than that. Don't tap it, whack it.
Tia hits it a little harder.
I'll tell you what, pretend the dashboard's my face. Okay?
Tia gives him a look. She takes him up on the suggestion. She draws back and gives the dashboard a thundering blow. The RADIO goes on LOUD.
EXT. HIGH SCHOOL. MORNING
A big, sprawling suburban high school: the lot. Buck's car pulls in
EXT. HIGH SCHOOL. MAIN ENTRANCE
Buck's Rivie~a approaches. The engine's roaring. A trail of blue smoke lingers behind.
EXT. SCHOOL. KIDS
Milling around the front door. High school kids. They look with revulsion as the Riviera rattles up the main drive.
EXT. CURB. RIVIERA
The brakes squeak like fingers coming down a chalkboard as the rolling embarrassment comes to a stop at the curb. It rumbles, shudders, shakes. The only person we see in the car is Buck.
INT. CAR. BUCK
He waits on the car as it goes through its shut-down ritual. He grimaces, holds the pained, anticipatory expression, holds out a finger ...
EXT. CAR
It's hissing. Something's going to blow.
EXT. SCHOOL. KIDS
They move back as they anticipate an explosion.
INT. CAR. BUCK
Holding his expression, grits his teeth, wags the finger and ELAM! A huge, wet backfire. He smiles.
(with sympathetic relief) Ahhh ... !
EXT. SCHOOL. KIDS
Scattering, rubbing their ears.
INT. CAR. BUCK
Buck looks down at the footwell.
Tying your shoe?
HIS POV Iia is hunched foreward to avoid being seen. She slowly sits up.
EXT. CAR
Tia's head rises ever so slowly in the window and she looks out fearful that she'll be seen.
He doesn't get it. He loves the old car and can't imagine it being an embarrassment.
What time do you want me to pick you up?
She turns to Buck. She's angry.
I'll get a ride.
INT. CAR. BUCK AND TIA
Tia gathers up her books and slips on sunglasses.
I got my orders.
What time?
Are you really this stupid? I said, I'll get a ride. I always get a ride.
I'll call the school, get the time and you meet me right here.
Call the school. I won't be here.
If you're ashamed to be seen in this car, you won't want me inside asking around about you. The car's a lot prettier than I am, don't you agree? You be here.
Tia stares angrily at Buck. She knows she's lost but won't admit it.
You stand me up and tomorrow morning, we'll arrive with the top down.
Tia doesn't react. Buck gives her a little extra incentive.
When I ride with the top down, I put zinc on my nose and wear a cowboy hat.
Tia glares at him. She thinks he's being deliberately cruel. She reaches for the door handle. Buck reaches under the seat for a pair of pliers.
Four o'clock?
I can't wait.
He hands her the pliers. She snatches them from him.
Did you ever have anybody embarrass you like this?
Not since I learned to do it myself.
I can't believe I'm related to you.
